title: PANDAS: Improving Many-shot Jailbreaking via Positive Affirmation, Negative Demonstration, and Adaptive Sampling

reviews.summary: The paper proposes PANDAS, a hybrid improvement to many-shot jailbreaking (MSJ) that combines three simple but effective modifications: (i) Positive Affirmations (PA) inserted between demonstrations to strengthen instruction-following; (ii) Negative Demonstrations (ND) that show an initial refusal followed by a user correction and then a harmful answer; and (iii) Adaptive Sampling (AS) of demonstration topics via Bayesian optimization conditioned on the target prompt’s topic. The authors also introduce ManyHarm, a curated dataset of 2,400 malicious Q/A demonstrations, and present extensive evaluations on five recent open-source models, two standard harmful-target datasets, ablations, analysis against defenses, and an attention-based analysis suggesting that PA/ND increase cross-segment “reference” attention to prior demonstrations.
strengths: The paper identifies and validates two lightweight prompt-level modifications (PA and ND) that individually and jointly boost MSJ without increasing the number of demonstrations.
Adaptive Sampling via Bayesian optimization over demonstration-topic distributions is a concrete and interesting way to exploit topic-conditioned transfer in long-context attacks.
The structured attention-partitioning and “reference score” metric provide an interpretable lens on how long-context prompts can bias attention toward prior demos.
Broad empirical coverage: five recent open-source models spanning long- and shorter-context windows, two widely used datasets (AdvBench, HarmBench), and multiple shot counts up to 256.
Clear ablations isolate the effects of PA, ND, and AS, and the combined variant typically outperforms MSJ and i-MSJ under the same demo count.
Defense evaluation (Self-Reminder, ICD variants, PPL filters, Retokenization, SmoothLLM) gives a balanced view of residual robustness in realistic settings.
The method is clearly defined, with explicit constructions for PA/ND and a precise description of the BO-based AS objective.
The attention-partitioning framework is well explained with helpful figures, and the “reference score” is intuitive.
Many-shot jailbreaking is highly consequential; simple, low-overhead improvements that consistently raise ASR highlight remaining gaps in current safety alignment.
ManyHarm (even if released upon request) can seed further community analysis of long-context vulnerabilities and topic interactions.
weaknesses: PA likely increases total token budget and conversational regularity; improvements may partially stem from a length/recency effect rather than purely “affirmation” semantics. There is no control where the same token budget is added via neutral filler to test this.

ND is closely related to ICD-style defenses; results suggest that similar structures can backfire when phrased differently. A deeper analysis of when/why “defensive” NDs help attackers (and how to avoid this) is needed.
The BO objective proxies LLM-judged ASR using first-token heuristics (“I” or “1”), which may be misaligned with the final safety outcome and could bias the learned distributions.

Adaptive Sampling optimization appears to be tuned per model and topic using the same datasets used for evaluation; no clear train/validation split within a topic is described to establish generalization to fresh targets.
i-MSJ is evaluated only on AdvBench50 due to compute, while PANDAS is reported across full datasets; this asymmetry undercuts the strength of baseline comparisons on the larger testbeds.
Reliance on a single LLM-as-a-judge (Llama-Guard-3) risks evaluation bias; the paper notes manual inspection but does not include multi-judge triangulation or human raters for a subset.

The selection of PA/ND insertion positions is tuned to maximize 256-shot ASR on AdvBench50, which serves as a test set rather than a held-out validation set; this should be clarified and, ideally, re-run with a proper dev split.
Some implementation details of BO (e.g., number of prompts per topic used during optimization, data splits, and compute cost) are relegated to the appendix and remain opaque.

Recent findings that MSJ effectiveness can be largely content-agnostic and driven by context/window phenomena (e.g., studies showing lorem ipsum or repeated safe shots can be comparably effective) are not discussed in depth, limiting the positioning of PANDAS relative to content-free or trivially generated attacks.
Broader surveys/taxonomies and cognitive-load-based jailbreak variants could contextualize this work within emerging long-context attack paradigms and highlight complementary or competing mechanisms.
reviews.questions:
1. How was Adaptive Sampling tuned per topic—did you use a held-out split distinct from the targets used for final evaluation, and if so, what was the split size and protocol? If not, can you report results with proper train/dev/test partitioning within each topic?
2. For PA and ND, can you provide token-budget controls (e.g., insert length-matched neutral/filler phrases) to rule out improvements stemming primarily from longer inputs or added structure rather than semantics?
3. How well does the BO proxy objective (first-token heuristics) correlate with final ASR-L/ASR-R across topics and models? Can you report correlation coefficients or calibration plots?
4. Could you expand i-MSJ comparisons to the full datasets under a matched compute budget, or alternatively cap PANDAS’s optimization (AS steps) to match i-MSJ’s search cost for a fairer comparison?
5. What is the generalization of the learned topic distributions: do they transfer across datasets (e.g., HarmBench→AdvBench topics) and to unseen prompts within a topic without re-optimizing?
6. When categorizing AdvBench prompts with Llama-3.1-8B, did you validate category quality (e.g., with human checks or inter-model agreement)? How sensitive is AS performance to categorization noise?
7. Will ManyHarm be publicly released under a governed access policy by camera-ready? If not, can you provide a redacted or sanitized surrogate that still supports replication of your key findings?
reviews.assessment: This is a timely, well-executed study that offers practical, low-overhead improvements to many-shot jailbreaking and provides useful mechanistic intuition via attention analysis. The empirical coverage is broad and the ablations are informative. However, several methodological issues limit the conclusiveness of the claims: the absence of token-budget controls for PA/ND, potential overfitting or evaluation leakage in Adaptive Sampling (no explicit held-out split per topic), reliance on a single judge LLM, and asymmetric baseline comparisons for i-MSJ. The novelty is moderate—PA/ND are simple engineering tweaks and BO-based sampling is a reasonable but not conceptually deep addition—but the demonstrated, consistent gains across models/datasets and the defense analysis make this work valuable to the community. With clarifications and added controls/splits, this paper would be a strong contribution. I lean toward acceptance conditional on addressing the evaluation rigor concerns, particularly the AS tuning protocol and controls for PA/ND.
